text.fullText:
Date: 01/30/2026
To: U.S. Environmental Protection Agency
Attention: Docket ID No. EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929
RE: Comments on Proposed Rule: Updating the Water Quality Certification Regulations (CWA Section 401)

To Whom It May Concern,
I am writing as a concerned citizen Florida/Flagler County to express my strong opposition to the proposed revisions to the Clean Water Act (CWA) Section 401 Water Quality Certification regulations. Specifically, I am deeply concerned by the proposal to narrow the "scope of certification" from “activity as a whole" to a "discharge-only" focus. This change significantly undermines the ability of States and Tribes to protect local waters from the cumulative environmental impacts of large-scale projects.
1. Loss of Oversight for Cumulative Effects By limiting the scope of review to the specific point of discharge, the proposed rule ignores the broader reality of how large infrastructure projects—such as pipelines and dams—impact our ecosystems. Water quality does not exist in a vacuum; it is influenced by upland construction, clearing of riparian vegetation, and changes in local hydrology. Narrowing the review prevents states from assessing the cumulative effects of a project’s entire lifecycle, leaving our communities vulnerable to "death by a thousand cuts" where each individual discharge point may seem minor, but the project’s total impact is devastating.
2. Threats to Local Streams and Wetlands The proposed rule creates a dangerous gap in the protection of our most vulnerable water bodies. Wetlands and small headwater streams are the biological "kidneys" of our watersheds. Under the proposed "discharge-only" scope, a state may be forced to certify a project even if the construction of the project itself results in the destruction of non-jurisdictional wetlands or the degradation of streams through non-point source runoff.
•Specifically, to Flagler County we have the Matanzas River Basin and local wetlands. It is one of the last relatively undisturbed estuaries of Florida’s east coast, with nearly 35% of the basin comprised of critical wetlands.
•Cumulative Development Pressure: With major land development projects currently sited in the headwaters of Pellicer, Moultrie, and Moses creeks, our watershed is already facing a projected increase in developed land use to over 25%. Under the proposed rule, the state would be unable to look at the: "activity as a whole" for these massive developments. This means they could be forced to ignore the cumulative loss of wetlands that act as our primary defense against storm surge and flooding—an issue that already causes "Moderate Flooding" levels (over 2.3 ft) at locations like Bings Landing.
•Wastewater and Infrastructure Strain: Palm Coast is currently grappling with wastewater capacity challenges, specifically at Wastewater Treatment Plant 1, which is nearing its permitted limits. By narrowing the scope of Section 401, the EPA would limit the ability of state regulators to ensure that new federal permits for large-scale projects do not exacerbate our existing infrastructure strain or lead to increased nutrient loading (nitrogen and phosphorus) that fuels harmful algal blooms in our canals and the Intracoastal Waterway.
•Pollution and Runoff: My community already faces threats from stormwater runoff carrying pesticides, sediments, and bacteria into our waterways. The proposed rule would prevent the state from placing necessary conditions on a project’s construction phase (like upland erosion control) simply because those impacts aren't tied to a specific "point source" pipe. This will inevitably lead to more pollution in our local streams and the degradation of the delicate salt marshes that protect our coastline.

3. Erosion of Cooperative Federalism Section 401 was designed by Congress to ensure that federal licenses do not bypass state water quality standards. This proposal shifts the balance of power away from the local experts and communities who best understand their own watersheds and hands it back to federal agencies that may prioritize project speed over local environmental health.
Conclusion I urge the EPA to maintain the 2023 Rule's "activity as a whole" scope. Protecting our water requires a holistic approach that considers how a project actually functions in the real world, not just how it looks at the end of a single pipe. I ask the Agency to prioritize the health of our local streams, wetlands, and families over regulatory shortcuts for industry.
Sincerely,
Brittany Myers
